# MSBuild Project Review: ContosoApp Solution

## Repository Overview

| Item | Details |
|------|---------|
| **Solution** | `ContosoApp.sln` (5 projects) |
| **Source projects** | `ContosoApp.Core`, `ContosoApp.Services`, `ContosoApp.Api` |
| **Test projects** | `ContosoApp.Core.Tests`, `ContosoApp.Api.Tests` |
| **Build infrastructure files** | `Directory.Build.props`: ❌ missing, `Directory.Build.targets`: ❌ missing, `Directory.Packages.props`: ❌ missing, `global.json`: ❌ missing, `.editorconfig`: ❌ missing |

All 5 projects are SDK-style (`Microsoft.NET.Sdk` / `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web`) targeting `net8.0`. That's a solid foundation — no legacy migration needed. But the infrastructure around them has significant duplication and drift.

---

## 🔴 Errors (Likely Broken or Risk of Build Failures)

### 🔴 E-1: Package Version Drift — `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions`

The same package is referenced at **two different versions** across projects:

| Project | Version |
|---------|---------|
| `ContosoApp.Core` | **8.0.0** |
| `ContosoApp.Services` | **8.0.1** |
| `ContosoApp.Api` | **8.0.0** |

`ContosoApp.Api` references both `Core` (8.0.0) and `Services` (8.0.1) as `ProjectReference` dependencies. NuGet will resolve this at build time, but this kind of silent version divergence causes difficult-to-diagnose assembly binding issues at runtime, especially under trimming or when publishing self-contained apps. It's a ticking time bomb.

**Fix:** Adopt Central Package Management (see Suggestion S-2 below), or at minimum align all references to the same version (`8.0.1`).

---

### 🔴 E-2: Test Project Package Version Skew

The two test projects use **different versions of the same test infrastructure packages**:

| Package | `Core.Tests` | `Api.Tests` |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` | **17.10.0** | **17.9.0** |
| `xunit` | **2.8.1** | **2.7.1** |
| `xunit.runner.visualstudio` | **2.8.1** | **2.5.6** |

This is a classic "organically grown" symptom — each test project was updated independently. Mismatched test SDK + runner versions can cause test discovery failures, different assertion behavior, or silent test-runner incompatibilities.

**Fix:** Centralize package versions (see S-2) and align to the latest versions.

---

## 🟡 Warnings (Anti-patterns That Should Be Fixed)

### 🟡 W-1: Massive Property Duplication Across All 5 Projects

Every single `.csproj` repeats these **7 identical properties**:

```xml
<TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
<Nullable>enable</Nullable>
<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
<EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
<Company>Contoso</Company>
<Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
```

This is a textbook case for `Directory.Build.props`. When the team decides to update to `net9.0`, they must touch **5 files**. If someone forgets one, you get a mixed-TFM build with subtle runtime errors.

**Fix:** Extract to a new `Directory.Build.props` at the repo root (see S-1).

---

### 🟡 W-2: Duplicated Custom Target `PrintBuildInfo` in 3 Source Projects

All three source projects (`Core`, `Services`, `Api`) contain an identical custom target:

```xml
<Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build">
  <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
</Target>
```

This target is copy-pasted verbatim. If you ever want to change the message format, you must update 3 files.

**Fix:** Move this to a `Directory.Build.targets` file at the repo root (or under `src/`). Targets that run during the build phase belong in `.targets`, not `.props`.

---

### 🟡 W-3: Duplicated `DocumentationFile` Property in 3 Source Projects

All three source projects repeat:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

**Fix:** Move to `Directory.Build.props` (or conditionally via `Directory.Build.targets` if you only want it for non-test projects). Since this is a property and not conditional on `$(TargetFramework)`, `.props` is fine.

---

### 🟡 W-4: Custom Target `PrintBuildInfo` Has No `Inputs`/`Outputs`

The `PrintBuildInfo` target uses `BeforeTargets="Build"` but declares no `Inputs` or `Outputs`. This means it **runs on every build invocation**, even on no-op builds when nothing has changed. This doesn't break anything for a simple `<Message>` task, but it's worth noting as a pattern violation — if this target ever gets heavier work, it will break incremental build.

---

### 🟡 W-5: Split `PropertyGroup` Elements in Source Projects

In `Core`, `Services`, and `Api`, the `DocumentationFile` property is in a **separate** `<PropertyGroup>` from the main one:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
  ...
</PropertyGroup>
<!-- ... items ... -->
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>...</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

There's no condition that justifies the split. Multiple unconditional `<PropertyGroup>` blocks reduce readability.

**Fix:** Once consolidated into `Directory.Build.props`, this goes away naturally.

---

## 🔵 Suggestions (Improvements for Maintainability & Performance)

### 🔵 S-1: Create `Directory.Build.props` at Repository Root

This single file eliminates W-1, W-3, and W-5. Recommended content:

```xml
<Project>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <Company>Contoso</Company>
    <Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- XML documentation for source projects only (test projects set IsPackable=false) -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(IsPackable)' != 'false'">
    <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>
```

After this, `ContosoApp.Core.csproj` shrinks from 26 lines to:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" />
    <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

And each test project shrinks to just its test packages + project reference:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" />
    <PackageReference Include="NSubstitute" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\..\src\ContosoApp.Core\ContosoApp.Core.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

---

### 🔵 S-2: Adopt Central Package Management (CPM) via `Directory.Packages.props`

This eliminates E-1, E-2, and all future version drift. Create at repo root:

```xml
<Project>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- Shared packages -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="8.0.5" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />

    <!-- Test packages -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.10.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.8.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="NSubstitute" Version="5.1.0" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

Then **remove all `Version="..."` attributes** from `<PackageReference>` elements in every `.csproj`. The `.csproj` files become:

```xml
<PackageReference Include="xunit" />       <!-- version comes from Directory.Packages.props -->
```

This gives you a single file to audit and update all package versions from, and `dotnet outdated` or Dependabot can target it directly.

---

### 🔵 S-3: Create `Directory.Build.targets` for Shared Targets

Move the `PrintBuildInfo` target from 3 source projects to a single file:

```xml
<Project>

  <Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build">
    <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
  </Target>

</Project>
```

Place at repo root (applies to all projects) or at `src/Directory.Build.targets` if you only want it for source projects.

---

### 🔵 S-4: Add a `global.json` to Pin SDK Version

There is no `global.json`. This means every developer and CI agent may use a different .NET SDK version, leading to "works on my machine" drift.

```json
{
  "sdk": {
    "version": "8.0.400",
    "rollForward": "latestFeature"
  }
}
```

---

### 🔵 S-5: Add an `.editorconfig` for Consistent Code Style

`EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` is `true` in every project — but there is no `.editorconfig` defining the rules. Without it, `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` enforces the **default** rules, which may not match team preferences and will differ between developers' IDE settings.

---

### 🔵 S-6: Missing `PrivateAssets="all"` on `xunit.runner.visualstudio`

In both test projects:
```xml
<PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1" />
```

`xunit.runner.visualstudio` is a test runner adapter — it should **never** flow to downstream consumers. Best practice:

```xml
<PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" PrivateAssets="all" />
```

Same applies to `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk`. These are build/test-time-only dependencies.

---

## 🟢 Positive (Things Done Well)

- ✅ **All projects are SDK-style** — no legacy migration needed
- ✅ **`TreatWarningsAsErrors` is enabled everywhere** — enforces code quality
- ✅ **`Nullable` and `ImplicitUsings` are consistently enabled** — modern C# features
- ✅ **`EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` is enabled** — build-time style enforcement
- ✅ **XML documentation generation is on for source projects** — good API documentation practice
- ✅ **Test projects have `IsPackable` set to `false`** — prevents accidental test NuGet publishing
- ✅ **No `packages.config` files** — already on `PackageReference`
- ✅ **Clean project references** — the dependency graph (`Api` → `Services` → `Core`) is clear and appropriate

---

## Summary of Recommended Changes

| Priority | Issue | Fix | Files Affected |
|----------|-------|-----|----------------|
| 🔴 High | Package version drift (E-1, E-2) | Create `Directory.Packages.props` with CPM | All 5 `.csproj` + 1 new file |
| 🟡 Medium | 7 duplicated properties (W-1) | Create `Directory.Build.props` | All 5 `.csproj` + 1 new file |
| 🟡 Medium | Duplicated target (W-2) | Create `Directory.Build.targets` | 3 source `.csproj` + 1 new file |
| 🟡 Medium | Duplicated DocumentationFile (W-3) | Move to `Directory.Build.props` | 3 source `.csproj` |
| 🔵 Low | Missing `global.json` (S-4) | Create `global.json` | 1 new file |
| 🔵 Low | Missing `.editorconfig` (S-5) | Create `.editorconfig` | 1 new file |
| 🔵 Low | Missing `PrivateAssets` (S-6) | Add `PrivateAssets="all"` | 2 test `.csproj` |

Implementing S-1 + S-2 + S-3 together would reduce the **total line count across all `.csproj` files from ~131 lines to ~52 lines** — a 60% reduction — while eliminating all version drift risk and making framework upgrades a one-line change.

# MSBuild Project Review: ContosoApp Solution

## Repository Overview

| Item | Details |
|------|---------|
| **Solution** | `ContosoApp.sln` (5 projects) |
| **Source projects** | `ContosoApp.Core`, `ContosoApp.Services`, `ContosoApp.Api` |
| **Test projects** | `ContosoApp.Core.Tests`, `ContosoApp.Api.Tests` |
| **Build infrastructure files** | `Directory.Build.props`: ❌ missing, `Directory.Build.targets`: ❌ missing, `Directory.Packages.props`: ❌ missing, `global.json`: ❌ missing, `.editorconfig`: ❌ missing |

All 5 projects are SDK-style (`Microsoft.NET.Sdk` / `Microsoft.NET.Sdk.Web`) targeting `net8.0`. That's a solid foundation — no legacy migration needed. But the infrastructure around them has significant duplication and drift.

---

## 🔴 Errors (Likely Broken or Risk of Build Failures)

### 🔴 E-1: Package Version Drift — `Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions`

The same package is referenced at **two different versions** across projects:

| Project | Version |
|---------|---------|
| `ContosoApp.Core` | **8.0.0** |
| `ContosoApp.Services` | **8.0.1** |
| `ContosoApp.Api` | **8.0.0** |

`ContosoApp.Api` references both `Core` (8.0.0) and `Services` (8.0.1) as `ProjectReference` dependencies. NuGet will resolve this at build time, but this kind of silent version divergence causes difficult-to-diagnose assembly binding issues at runtime, especially under trimming or when publishing self-contained apps. It's a ticking time bomb.

**Fix:** Adopt Central Package Management (see Suggestion S-2 below), or at minimum align all references to the same version (`8.0.1`).

---

### 🔴 E-2: Test Project Package Version Skew

The two test projects use **different versions of the same test infrastructure packages**:

| Package | `Core.Tests` | `Api.Tests` |
|---------|-------------|-------------|
| `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk` | **17.10.0** | **17.9.0** |
| `xunit` | **2.8.1** | **2.7.1** |
| `xunit.runner.visualstudio` | **2.8.1** | **2.5.6** |

This is a classic "organically grown" symptom — each test project was updated independently. Mismatched test SDK + runner versions can cause test discovery failures, different assertion behavior, or silent test-runner incompatibilities.

**Fix:** Centralize package versions (see S-2) and align to the latest versions.

---

## 🟡 Warnings (Anti-patterns That Should Be Fixed)

### 🟡 W-1: Massive Property Duplication Across All 5 Projects

Every single `.csproj` repeats these **7 identical properties**:

```xml
<TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
<Nullable>enable</Nullable>
<ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
<TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
<EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
<Company>Contoso</Company>
<Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
```

This is a textbook case for `Directory.Build.props`. When the team decides to update to `net9.0`, they must touch **5 files**. If someone forgets one, you get a mixed-TFM build with subtle runtime errors.

**Fix:** Extract to a new `Directory.Build.props` at the repo root (see S-1).

---

### 🟡 W-2: Duplicated Custom Target `PrintBuildInfo` in 3 Source Projects

All three source projects (`Core`, `Services`, `Api`) contain an identical custom target:

```xml
<Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build">
  <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
</Target>
```

This target is copy-pasted verbatim. If you ever want to change the message format, you must update 3 files.

**Fix:** Move this to a `Directory.Build.targets` file at the repo root (or under `src/`). Targets that run during the build phase belong in `.targets`, not `.props`.

---

### 🟡 W-3: Duplicated `DocumentationFile` Property in 3 Source Projects

All three source projects repeat:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

**Fix:** Move to `Directory.Build.props` (or conditionally via `Directory.Build.targets` if you only want it for non-test projects). Since this is a property and not conditional on `$(TargetFramework)`, `.props` is fine.

---

### 🟡 W-4: Custom Target `PrintBuildInfo` Has No `Inputs`/`Outputs`

The `PrintBuildInfo` target uses `BeforeTargets="Build"` but declares no `Inputs` or `Outputs`. This means it **runs on every build invocation**, even on no-op builds when nothing has changed. This doesn't break anything for a simple `<Message>` task, but it's worth noting as a pattern violation — if this target ever gets heavier work, it will break incremental build.

---

### 🟡 W-5: Split `PropertyGroup` Elements in Source Projects

In `Core`, `Services`, and `Api`, the `DocumentationFile` property is in a **separate** `<PropertyGroup>` from the main one:

```xml
<PropertyGroup>
  <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
  ...
</PropertyGroup>
<!-- ... items ... -->
<PropertyGroup>
  <DocumentationFile>...</DocumentationFile>
</PropertyGroup>
```

There's no condition that justifies the split. Multiple unconditional `<PropertyGroup>` blocks reduce readability.

**Fix:** Once consolidated into `Directory.Build.props`, this goes away naturally.

---

## 🔵 Suggestions (Improvements for Maintainability & Performance)

### 🔵 S-1: Create `Directory.Build.props` at Repository Root

This single file eliminates W-1, W-3, and W-5. Recommended content:

```xml
<Project>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <TargetFramework>net8.0</TargetFramework>
    <Nullable>enable</Nullable>
    <ImplicitUsings>enable</ImplicitUsings>
    <TreatWarningsAsErrors>true</TreatWarningsAsErrors>
    <EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>true</EnforceCodeStyleInBuild>
    <Company>Contoso</Company>
    <Authors>Contoso Engineering</Authors>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <!-- XML documentation for source projects only (test projects set IsPackable=false) -->
  <PropertyGroup Condition="'$(IsPackable)' != 'false'">
    <DocumentationFile>$(OutputPath)$(AssemblyName).xml</DocumentationFile>
  </PropertyGroup>

</Project>
```

After this, `ContosoApp.Core.csproj` shrinks from 26 lines to:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" />
    <PackageReference Include="System.Text.Json" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

And each test project shrinks to just its test packages + project reference:

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">

  <PropertyGroup>
    <IsPackable>false</IsPackable>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit" />
    <PackageReference Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" />
    <PackageReference Include="NSubstitute" />
  </ItemGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <ProjectReference Include="..\..\src\ContosoApp.Core\ContosoApp.Core.csproj" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

---

### 🔵 S-2: Adopt Central Package Management (CPM) via `Directory.Packages.props`

This eliminates E-1, E-2, and all future version drift. Create at repo root:

```xml
<Project>

  <PropertyGroup>
    <ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>true</ManagePackageVersionsCentrally>
  </PropertyGroup>

  <ItemGroup>
    <!-- Shared packages -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.Extensions.Logging.Abstractions" Version="8.0.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="System.Text.Json" Version="8.0.5" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Newtonsoft.Json" Version="13.0.3" />
    <PackageVersion Include="Swashbuckle.AspNetCore" Version="6.5.0" />

    <!-- Test packages -->
    <PackageVersion Include="Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk" Version="17.10.0" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit" Version="2.8.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="xunit.runner.visualstudio" Version="2.8.1" />
    <PackageVersion Include="NSubstitute" Version="5.1.0" />
  </ItemGroup>

</Project>
```

Then **remove all `Version="..."` attributes** from `<PackageReference>` elements in every `.csproj`. The `.csproj` files become:

```xml
<PackageReference Include="xunit" />       <!-- version comes from Directory.Packages.props -->
```

This gives you a single file to audit and update all package versions from, and `dotnet outdated` or Dependabot can target it directly.

---

### 🔵 S-3: Create `Directory.Build.targets` for Shared Targets

Move the `PrintBuildInfo` target from 3 source projects to a single file:

```xml
<Project>

  <Target Name="PrintBuildInfo" BeforeTargets="Build">
    <Message Importance="high" Text="Building $(AssemblyName) [$(Configuration)]" />
  </Target>

</Project>
```

Place at repo root (applies to all projects) or at `src/Directory.Build.targets` if you only want it for source projects.

---

### 🔵 S-4: Add a `global.json` to Pin SDK Version

There is no `global.json`. This means every developer and CI agent may use a different .NET SDK version, leading to "works on my machine" drift.

```json
{
  "sdk": {
    "version": "8.0.400",
    "rollForward": "latestFeature"
  }
}
```

---

### 🔵 S-5: Add an `.editorconfig` for Consistent Code Style

`EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` is `true` in every project — but there is no `.editorconfig` defining the rules. Without it, `EnforceCodeStyleInBuild` enforces the **default** rules, which may not match team preferences and will differ between developers' IDE settings.

---

### 🔵 S-6: Missing `PrivateAssets="all"` on `xunit.runner.visualstudio`

In both test projects:

```

`xunit.runner.visualstudio` is a test runner adapter — it should **never** flow to downstream consumers. Best practice:

```xml

```

Same applies to `Microsoft.NET.Test.Sdk`. These are build/test-time-only dependencies.

---

- ✅ **All projects are SDK-style** — no legacy migration needed
- ✅ **`TreatWarningsAsErrors` is enabled everywhere** — enforces code quality

| 🔴 High | Package version drift (E-1, E-2) | Create `Directory.Packages.props` with CPM | All 5 `.csproj` + 1 new file |
